title: Are We to Remain Silent Witnesses?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored, the corrupted historical year has been verified against the printed page, and clear OCR word-break and spelling errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: For many years the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam has explained the indispensable importance of mother-tongue education and worked for it. More particularly, education in the mother tongue is one of the movement's vital principles. Every Tamil must develop the understanding and determination that service to our mother tongue, Tamil, is an inborn duty.

Mahatma Gandhi himself advised: “Education through the mother tongue is best suited to a child's mental development. Imposing any other language on students is a sin committed against the motherland.” Whenever the DMK governed, it worked to give mother-tongue education primacy. Yet a particular section repeatedly obstructed it from achieving complete success. The obsession with English has grown so strong that it prevents mother-tongue education from taking deep root and flourishing.

Powerful countries such as China, France, Germany and Japan do not submit to English dominance. They educate through their own languages. When students pursue higher education in their mother tongue, they understand what they study better and advance faster.

Reports now say that medical students in Punjab will no longer need to read their medical books in English because the Government has decided to translate them into Punjabi. Punjab has accepted that studying in the mother tongue improves comprehension and continually sharpens knowledge. Under a Government order, the health sciences wing of Baba Farid University has begun translating medical books into its own language.
Sixty doctors have reportedly been assigned to this work. Once it is complete, Punjab's medical students will read all their medical texts in their own mother tongue.
Tamil Nadu must take the same decision.

The DMK Government worked towards such a goal. As a first step, in June 2010 it made Tamil Nadu the first State in India to provide engineering education in the mother tongue, introducing Tamil-medium civil and mechanical engineering at Anna University of Technology. That very year, 1,378 students joined Tamil-medium engineering courses.

After the DMK left office, students were not continuously encouraged to pursue engineering in Tamil. Had greater interest been shown, Tamil-medium teaching would have expanded to every engineering discipline and then to medicine and other professional courses. Because the programme was begun under a DMK Government, a stumbling block was placed in its way too.

University engineering examination papers had previously been issued only in English. From 2010-11, the DMK Government instructed universities to provide papers in English or Tamil and permit answers in either language. Tamil was also made a compulsory subject in undergraduate study, with students required to pass it. Thus the DMK Government began developing Tamil-medium education stage by stage.
When the DMK assumed office for the fifth time, it enacted and implemented a law on 31 May 2006 making Tamil-language education compulsory from Standard I through Standard X.
The DMK Government exempted Tamil-medium students in government and aided schools in Standards X and XII from paying public-examination fees, benefiting nearly ten lakh students each year.

For students' welfare, from the 2008-09 academic year it abolished the special fee paid by pupils in government and government-aided schools. The Government absorbed Rs 21.40 crore, benefiting nearly fifty lakh students annually.

As early as November 1970, the DMK cabinet decided that those educated through Tamil should receive first preference in employment under a Government whose official language is Tamil. Only positions remaining thereafter would go to those educated through English, while minority interests would remain protected.

Punjab's decision to permit medical education in the mother tongue follows this path. Pandit Nehru too said: “I am among those firmly convinced that a student's mother tongue is the most effective medium of instruction.” At least while celebrating Nehru's 125th birth anniversary, we should think deeply about his view and put it into practice.
Mother-tongue education is both easier and more natural. It can sharpen knowledge and enrich thought. We must now foster in our children a desire to study through their mother tongue.

That does not mean English should be rejected entirely. It may continue as a language subject. English-medium institutions have multiplied while enrolment in Tamil schools is declining. The use of English is gradually decreasing in many parts of the world. Even so, I have never opposed acquiring knowledge of English. At the same time, I cannot accept any argument against Tamil-medium education.

In the Tamil Nadu Assembly in 1968, Arignar Anna, then Chief Minister, passed a resolution declaring that Tamil would become the effective medium of instruction within five years. Though those five years have become fifty, the sorrow remains that Anna's resolution has still not been implemented.

Just as mother-tongue education is a vital principle, making Tamil an official language of India is another. At the launch of Professor A. Ramasamy's book in late January 2006, I said that every State official language should become an official language of India: Malayalam, Telugu, Punjabi and all the rest.

If anyone argues that this would cause too many complications, I would tell them: if all those languages have not yet come of age, Tamil has. It is a mature language waiting to be wedded to official status. Let the others be considered afterwards. First make Tamil, rich in every resource and endowed with grammar and literature, an official language. Our argument is not that other languages should be excluded. Make every State official language a common official language of India. Only then will Indian unity cease to be a bundle tied together and become something firm.
On this basis, every DMK election manifesto continues to press the demand for Tamil as an official language.

Like harnessing a horse behind the cart, the AIADMK Government has failed to advance Tamil-medium education and has dared to introduce English-medium teaching even in government primary schools. It will neither support nor encourage Tamil-medium education.
For how many more days will the people of Tamil Nadu remain silent witnesses, watching and enduring such regressive measures?
With affection,
M.K.
